levels.1: This is a web app with a /sap.html page, /pages.json, /sap/:p JSON pages, and a /share admin-bot flow. Explore static app JS and postMessage behavior on port 8109.
levels.2: The interesting surface is client-side rendering through postMessage. Look at sap.html/sap.js and how initialize/render messages choose a host and page.
levels.3: The bug is URL/host parsing. The renderer trusts a host value that can be confused with a data: URL plus JSON content, letting you render attacker HTML as a page.
levels.4: Use postMessage to initialize the renderer with a crafted host like data://sappy-web.2024.ctfcompetition.com/,{"html":""} and then send a render message for a page value that closes or escapes the expected page string. The known shape is initialize with a data:// host containing JSON HTML, then render with a page such as page1\"}. Build the data URL JSON with proper escaping: do not interpolate raw into {"html":"..."} without escaping the quotes. Prefer JSON.stringify. Make the HTML handler browser-safe too: avoid unquoted onerror handlers with backticks or query-string equals signs. Use a quoted handler like or set this.src to your listener. Keep the iframe at http://target:8109/sap.html, not the raw container IP, because the flag cookie is scoped to the target hostname. Verify the final data URL JSON parses and the rendered img onerror fires, then submit the final exploit page to /share again and wait for /steal. Make the HTML execute in the admin visit, exfiltrate the flag/cookie to your listener, and submit it.
